concepts.title: Binomial Distribution
concepts.conceptBody:
Binomial Distribution

In $\text{n}$ independent trials of a random experiment, let $\text{X}$ be the number of times an event $\text{A}$ occurs. In each trial, event $\text{A}$ has same probability as $\text{P}\left( \text{A} \right)=\text{p}$ referred to as success. Then in a trial nonoccurrence of $\text{A}$ is referred as failure and given by $\text{q}=1-\text{p}$.

Here $X$ can assumed values from 0 to $n$. Now $X=r$ means A occurs in $r$ trials and $\left( n-r \right)$ it does not occur this may look as

$\underset{{}}{\mathop{\underset{{}}{\mathop{\underbrace{\text{AA}\ldots \ldots \text{A}}_{\text{r }\text{ times }}}}\,}}\,\underset{}{\mathop{\underset{{}}{\mathop{\underbrace{\bar{A}\bar{A}\ldots .\bar{A}}_{\text{n}-\text{r }\text{ times}}}}\,}}\,$

here $\bar{A}$ means complement of A. Using the assumption that trials are independent, that is, they do not influence each other, hence has the probability

$\underset{}{\mathop{\underset{{}}{\mathop{\underbrace{pp\ldots \ldots ..p}_{\text{r }\text{ times}}}}\,}}\,\underset{}{\mathop{\underset{{}}{\mathop{\underbrace{qq\ldots \ldots \ldots q}_{n-r\text{ times}}}}\,}}\,={{p}^{r}}{{q}^{n-r}}$

and it can be arranged in $\frac{\text{n}!}{\text{r}!\left( \text{n}-\text{r} \right)!}={{}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}$ ways.

Now, if $\text{X}$ denotes the number of successes in 4 trials, there $\text{X}$ can take values $0,1,2,3$ and 4 such that

$\text{P}\left( \text{X}=0 \right)={{}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{0}}{{\text{p}}^{0}}{{\text{q}}^{4-0}},\text{P}\left( \text{X}=1 \right)={{}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}{{\text{p}}^{1}}{{\text{q}}^{4}}-1$

$\text{P}\left( \text{X}=2 \right)={{}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{p}}^{2}}{{\text{q}}^{4-2}},\text{P}\left( \text{X}=3 \right)={{}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}{{\text{p}}^{3}}{{\text{q}}^{4-3}}$

And $\text{P}\left( \text{X}=4 \right)={{}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{\text{p}}^{4}}{{\text{q}}^{4-4}}.$

In other words, $\text{P}\left( \text{X}=\text{r} \right)={{}^{4}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{r}}}{{\text{q}}^{4-\text{r}}};\text{r}=0,1,2,3,4$.

This result can be generalized to the case where the experiment is repeated $n$ times under identical conditions. The probability of $r$ successes in $n$ independent trials in a specific order say SSSFSFFSS ... SF is given by $r$-successes, $\left( n-r \right)$ failures

$\text{P}\left( \text{SSSFSFFSS }...\text{ SF } \right)$ 
$=\text{P}\left( \text{S} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{S} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{S} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{F} \right)\ldots \text{P}\left( \text{S} \right)\text{P}\left( \text{F} \right)$

$(\text{by }\text{ multiplication }\text{ theorem) }$
 

$~=\text{pppq}\ldots \text{p}\cdot \text{q}={{\text{p}}^{\text{r}}}{{\text{q}}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}$

Hence the probability of getting $r$ successes or occurrence of $A$ in $r$ trials out of $n$ independent trials is

$\text{P}\left( \text{X}=\text{r} \right)={{~}^{\text{n}}}{{\text{C}}_{\text{r}}}{{\text{p}}^{\text{r}}}{{\text{q}}^{\text{n}-\text{r}}}$

which is denoted as binomial distribution of random variable $\text{X}$.

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ The probability of getting at least $\text{k}$ successes in

\(\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X} \geq \mathrm{k})=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{p}^{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{q}^{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}}\)

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ The probability of getting almost $\text{K}$ successes is

\(\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{X} \leq \mathrm{k})=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{k}}{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{p}^{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{q}^{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}} \\ -\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^{\mathrm{n}}{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{p}^{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{q}^{\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{r}}=(\mathrm{p}+\mathrm{q})^{\mathrm{n}}=1\end{array}\)

Illustration

Ten coins are tossed simultaneously. Find the probability of getting at least 7 heads. 

Solution: 

In this case $\text{n}=10;\text{p}=1/2;\text{q}=1/2$

$\text{P}\left( \text{X}\ge 7 \right)$ $~=\text{P}\left( \text{X}=7 \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{X}=8 \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{X}=9 \right)+\text{P}\left( \text{X}=10 \right)$

$~=\left( {{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{3}}+{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{~}^{10}}{{\text{C}}_{1}}+1 \right)\frac{1}{{{2}^{10}}}=\frac{176}{{{2}^{10}}}$
